content:
Most SEO hosting buyers frame this as a price decision.
They compare the $40/mo VPS entry point against the $199/mo dedicated server entry point, subtract the difference, and either justify the upgrade or defer it.
That framing misses the actual variable. And once you understand the real one, the decision becomes much cleaner.
Both Tiers Access the Same IP Pool
This is the part that surprises most buyers: an ASEOHosting SEO VPS and an ASEOHosting Dedicated Server pull Class C IPs from the same pool. Over 800 unique Class C subnets, assigned exclusively — no address shared between accounts. The IP Manager Tool that reassigns any Class C to any domain in a single click? Identical on both tiers.
So the difference isn’t about IP access, IP quality, or IP tool functionality. Those are the same.
The difference is the server resource model underneath that IP network.
What the Cost Math Actually Shows
At 100 Class C IPs, the all-in monthly cost breaks down like this:

• SEO VPS (VPS-4, 100 IPs, cPanel): $465/mo

• Dedicated Server (entry, 100 IPs, cPanel): $719/mo
The VPS wins by $254/mo at that scale. It also delivers the best per-IP rate in the ASEOHosting catalog — $3.75/IP once you cross 50 IPs, which undercuts the dedicated server rate at comparable quantities.
But here’s where it gets interesting: the VPS-4 running 100 active domains with WHM and scheduled crawlers is operating near its resource ceiling. The dedicated server running the same workload has 16 GB of physical RAM and dedicated CPU cores — no KVM hypervisor time-slicing them against other tenants.
The question isn’t which tier costs less. It’s which tier has adequate resources for your actual workload at that scale.
The Upgrade Trigger Is Not What You Think
Most guides say to upgrade to dedicated when you hit a certain domain count. That’s not the right trigger.
The right trigger is crawl concurrency.
When 150 domains with active crawlers run simultaneously — link-verification scripts, monitoring pings, Screaming Frog sessions — the KVM scheduling layer becomes the bottleneck. The same 150 domains with scheduled (off-peak, staggered) crawls sit comfortably on VPS-4.
The domain count is a proxy. The actual constraint is how many of those domains are demanding server resources at the same moment.
What the Full Guide Covers
The full breakdown at ASEOHosting includes:

• A verified spec table comparing every dimension across both tiers — hardware, IP pricing at all volume bands, SLA, cPanel cost, domain scale, and crawl concurrency

• An interactive IP Budget Calculator: enter your target IP count, get the all-in monthly cost for both tiers instantly

• The exact cost crossover point where dedicated becomes the lower per-domain option

• A two-column decision checklist (six signals for each tier)

• Ten FAQ answers covering the scenarios buyers actually search — including how KVM overhead compounds under simultaneous crawl load, and whether tier choice affects Googlebot re-crawl speed after a Class C IP reassignment
The calculator alone is worth the visit if you’re in the 75–200 IP range where the cost picture is non-obvious.
